key_points: The angle subtended by a chord at the center is twice the angle subtended at any point on the remaining part of the circle.
The perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects the chord.
Equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the center.
The sum of the opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180°.
Tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius at the point of contact.
Two tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal in length.
formulas: Length of chord: l = 2√(r² - d²), where d = distance from center to chord
Length of tangent from external point P: PT = √(PA² - r²), PA = distance from center to P
Area of sector: A = (θ/360) × πr², θ in degrees
examples: A chord AB of length 8 cm is 3 cm from the center. Find the radius → r = √(d² + (l/2)²) = √(3² + 4²) = 5 cm
Two tangents from point P outside the circle are equal. If distance from center to P is 13 cm and radius 5 cm, find tangent length → √(13²-5²)=12 cm
Angle at center is 100°, angle on remaining circle = 100/2 = 50°
exam_tips: Use the perpendicular from center to chord for length or distance problems.
Always check if quadrilateral is cyclic to apply sum of opposite angles.
For tangent problems, radius ⊥ tangent at point of contact.
Angles in the same segment of a circle are equal.
Convert angles to radians if needed for arc length or sector area formulas.
